coredata.dc:title: Thiourea-functionalized graphene aerogel for the aqueous phase sensing of toxic Pb(II) metal ions and H2O2
coredata.dc:creator: Kaushik, Jaidev
Gunture,
Tripathi, Kumud Malika
Singh, Ravindra
Sonkar, Sumit Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A simpler approach of functionalization for the fabrication of thiourea-functionalized-Graphene Aerogel (t-GA) is described here. Graphene Aerogel (GA) was synthesized from bio-mass, which on a simpler oxidative treatment get converted to its water-soluble version due to the impregnation of several oxygenous functionalities like carboxylic, hydroxyl, etc. Further, these carboxylated groups have been functionalized with the molecules of thiourea using the long known dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) as a coupling agent. The as-synthesized t-GA shows bright yellow fluorescence with a quantum yield of ~3% and holds the high-aqueous solubility and photostability. The fluorescence property of t-GA has been used here for the specific and selective sensing of toxic lead (Pb(II)) metal ions from the used many other metal ions via the fluorescence quenching and showed a limit of detection ~7.3 nM. Further, the mechanism for selective sensing was studied in detail and found to be preferable via ligand to metal charge transfer quenching mechanism. The cyclic voltammetry studies supported the selective sensing of Pb(II). Moreover, t-GA has also been studied for the sensing of hydrogen peroxide and as a yellow fluorescent ink.

Fluorescent ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS analysis of t-GA showing (a) full survey showing the presence of various elements on the surface; (b) tabulated elemental composition of t-GA; and deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s; (d) O1s; (e) N1s; and (f) S2p.

Comparative (a) FTIR and (b) Raman analysis of o-GA (black line) and t-GA (green line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis (black line) and Fluorescence spectra (violet line) of t-GA; (b) Fluorescence emission stability of t-GA measured up to 3 h and inset is showing the photographic images of t-GA solution, in daylight and UV light; (c) fluorescence spectra of t-GA taken at the different excitation wavelength (from 300 to 480 nm with 20 nm increment); and (d) back excitation spectra of t-GA taken at a maximum emission wavelength of ~527 nm showing two excitation centers located at ~350 nm and ~467 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Low-resolution TEM image of t-GA (a and b) showing various rotated sheet-like structures; (c) HRTEM image of t-GA showing graphitic patterns of sheets-like morphology; and (d) HRTEM image of t-GA showing graphitic fringes and d-spacing.

(a) Effect of addition of different toxic metals on the Fluorescence emission spectra of t-GA; (b) Spectra of relative fluorescence intensity (I0/I) of t-GA after addition of different metal ions showing selective fluorescence quenching in case of Pb(II) metal ions; (c) UV light photographic images of t-GA after the addition of different metals showing no fluorescence in case of Pb(II) ions; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra taken with the addition of Pb(II) (0–720 μM); and (e) Stern-Volmer plot showing a linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Pb(II) ion concentration.

(a) Absorbance spectra of Pb(II) ions (red line) and t-GA (black line), Fluorescence excitation (pink line) and emission spectra (blue line) of t-GA; (b) mechanism of fluorescence quenching of t-GA via electron transfer to empty d-orbitals of Pb(II) metal ions; and (c) schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching of t-GA by complex formation with Pb(II) ions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The cyclic voltammograms of t-GA before and after the addition of Pb (II) metal ions supporting electrochemical sensing by Pb(II) metal ions using a three-electrode system.

(a) Fluorescence quenching based sensing of H2O2 with respect to H2O; (b) Titration of H2O2 with t-GA to check the effect of concentration on fluorescence quenching; and (c) Stern Volmer plot of relative fluorescence intensity with a concentration of H2O2.

Quantification analysis of unknown concentration by the comparative bar plot presentation of actual and experimentally calculated concentration of (a) Pb(II); and (b) H2O2; (c) fluorescence emission spectra of t-GA in presence of different water/wastewater samples; and (d) Bar plot showing the sensing ability of t-GA towards Pb(II) ions and H2O2 in the presence of different water/wastewater samples.
Pictures of TLC plate with alphabets ‘LEAD’ written on it, (a) in presence of daylight and (b) in UV light.
Schematic representation showing post-functionalization processes of o-GA by thiourea via DCC coupling to yield yellow-fluorescent t-GA.
Comparison of sensor based properties of t-GA with other recent reports.

head.ce:title: Thiourea-functionalized graphene aerogel for the aqueous phase sensing of toxic Pb(II) metal ions and H2O2

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simpler approach of functionalization for the fabrication of thiourea-functionalized-Graphene Aerogel (t-GA) is described here. Graphene Aerogel (GA) was synthesized from bio-mass, which on a simpler oxidative treatment get converted to its water-soluble version due to the impregnation of several oxygenous functionalities like carboxylic, hydroxyl, etc. Further, these carboxylated groups have been functionalized with the molecules of thiourea using the long known dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) as a coupling agent. The as-synthesized t-GA shows bright yellow fluorescence with a quantum yield of ~3% and holds the high-aqueous solubility and photostability. The fluorescence property of t-GA has been used here for the specific and selective sensing of toxic lead (Pb(II)) metal ions from the used many other metal ions via the fluorescence quenching and showed a limit of detection ~7.3 nM. Further, the mechanism for selective sensing was studied in detail and found to be preferable via ligand to metal charge transfer quenching mechanism. The cyclic voltammetry studies supported the selective sensing of Pb(II). Moreover, t-GA has also been studied for the sensing of hydrogen peroxide and as a yellow fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:para: Presently, methodologies related to identifying and removing hazardous materials, especially the dissolved toxic metal ions (Fu and Wang, 2011), dyes (Forgacs et al., 2004), pesticides (Ahmad et al., 2010), etc., from the usable water, hold the major scientific concern. Toxic metal ions like chromium (Cr(VI)) (Zhitkovich, 2011), mercury (Hg(II)) (Driscoll et al., 2013), lead (Pb(II)) (Kim et al., 2012), etc. are directly or indirectly harshening the environment and human health(Tripathi et al., 2016; Rahman and Singh, 2019). Toxic metal ions, like lead (Pb) holds a high profile as the most detrimental metal ion which causes various symptoms such as memory loss, mental retardation, anemia, and muscle paralysis (Chen and Huang, 2002; Xue et al., 2005; Flora et al., 2012). The release of Pb(II) ions into the environment is primarily associated with their wide range of uses (batteries, gasoline, paints industries, etc.) (Kim et al., 2012; Arif et al., 2019). So, development strategies related to the selective identification of Pb(II) metal ions followed by its removal (Mahar et al., 2019) are highly desirable. For the same, numerous methods for the qualitative and quantitative determination of Pb(II) in water sources have been used, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Islam et al., 2014) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) (Townsend et al., 1998). Apart from these expensive-sophisticated techniques, presently, a few newer and almost-inexpensive (Ramnani et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021) nano-materials-based optical absorption and emission techniques are used for the precise detections of toxic Pb(II) metal ions(Liu et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017a, 2017b). Fluorescence-based quenching methods are convenient, easier to use (Wang et al., 2016), and can detect even traces of contaminants(Kim et al., 2012). So, there is always a demand for inexpensive materials/nano-material that can identify and quantify the contents of heavy metal ions for real-time monitoring in biological, environmental, and industrial samples/effluents(Organization, 2003; Kim et al., 2012).

Concerning the involvement of fluorescence-based detection technique, functionalized nano-carbons (Anand et al., 2019) showed their précised-prominent sensing abilities(Aggarwal et al., 2019; Chauhan et al., 2019; Gunture et al., 2020). Herein, Graphene Aerogel (GA) has been chosen for the post functionalization processes to synthesize fluorescent GA. Various groups have explored the various properties of GA, such as their electrical conductivity (Worsley et al., 2010), elasticity (Deng et al., 2020), compressibilities (Niu et al., 2021), superhydrophobicity (Lin et al., 2011), adsorption capacity (Tang et al., 2019; Kaushik et al., 2021) etc. Pottathara et al. have briefly discussed the synthetic protocols of GA and used them as supercapacitors(Pottathara et al., 2020). Yu et al. prepared graphene oxide aerogel (GO-aerogel) and used them for the adsorption of heavy metal ions and toxic dyes(Yu et al., 2017). Liu et al. have used nano-FeO(OH)/reduced graphene oxide aerogel (rGO-aerogel) for the catalytic degradation of phenolic molecules in water(Liu et al., 2018). Fu et al. have fabricated GA from photoreduction of GO and used it for the harvesting of solar energy(Fu et al., 2017). Ye et al. have used paraffin encapsulated GA for the thermal energy storage application(Ye et al., 2015). GA bears a large surface area (Sui et al., 2014), with the availability of numerous sites that can provide great possibilities for surface functionalization. However, the reports related to the functionalization of GA are very limited in number but hold considerable potential for application point of view.

This work reports the simpler functionalization of GA via the molecules of thiourea. Thiourea functionalized graphene aerogel (t-GA) showed good yellow-fluorescence property which was applied for the specific selectivity towards Pb(II) metal ions than other heavy metal ions based on the experiment of fluorescence quenching. The sensing of Pb(II) was being supported by electrochemical responses that was analyzed via cyclic voltammetry experiments. Additionally, the t-GA was used for the sensing of hydrogen peroxide H2O2, which can act as an established mark for oxidative mutilation to the living body(Wu et al., 2014). A dilute solution of t-GA was also used as a fluorescent ink.

GA was converted to its oxidized carboxylated form (o-GA) via a simple oxidative functionalization using nitric acid(Kaushik et al., 2021). Afterward, the surface of o-GA was further functionalized with thiourea (as shown in Scheme 1 ) via the dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) coupling mechanism. The coupling procedure goes through an O-acylisourea intermediate (Iwasawa et al., 2007) formed after the reaction of DCC and o-GA, which on reaction with thiourea in basic medium gives t-GA. The unreacted thiourea and DCC molecules were removed by solvent washing. The surface functionalization by the thiourea molecules imparts good aqueous solubility and bright yellow fluorescence in t-GA.

The surface functionalization of t-GA has been analyzed by the X-Ray Photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy to get information about the elemental composition and types of chemical bondings present over the surface of t-GA. Fig. 1 a is the XPS survey scan of t-GA showing five characteristic peaks at ~167.5, ~231.0, ~283.5, ~398.5 and ~530.5 eV which can be related to bonding configuration of S2
p , S2
s , C1s, N1s and O1s , respectively. Fig. 1b is showing the tabulated percentage elemental composition of C, O, N and S, which are ~67.37%, ~21.28%, ~7.28% and ~4.07%, respectively. It can be seen that after the functionalization with thiourea N/S ratio was found to be ~1.8 which is nearly equal to 2 as for thiourea. Fig. 1c is the deconvoluted C1s short scan spectra showing peaks at 283.9, 284.3, 285.0, and 288.0 eV, which correspond to CC, C–C, C–O, CO/S, respectively. The carbonyl and thiocarbonyl groups were having the same environment (i.e. C(O/S)–N) and were not distinguishable. Fig. 1d is the deconvoluted short scan spectra of O1s representing two peaks at ~530.8 eV and ~531.6 eV, which are related to C–O/N and CO/C(O)–N bonds, respectively. Deconvulated spectra of N1s are showing peaks at ~398.7, ~399.3, and ~400.9 eV, which may relate to three different bonding environments of nitrogen i.e. C(O)–N (amidic), C–N–H, and C–N–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 1e). While deconvoluted short spectra of S2p is showing two peaks located at ~163.3 eV and ~168.0 eV (Qu et al., 2013; Qiu et al., 2019). First peak corresponds to the characteristic low oxidized covalently bonded sulfur i.e. CS, while the other peak is related to the highly oxidized sulfur i.e. due to the formation of oxides of sulfur (Chen et al., 2020) (Fig. 1f). Oxidation of sulfur may occur due to various oxygenated moieties present on the surface of o-GA in the form of hydroxyl and carboxylate groups(Liu et al., 2011). Thus, XPS analysis confirms the proper functionalization of thiourea attached to the carboxylated group present on the surface via amidic linkages.

The functionalization of t-GA has been supported by a comparative FTIR and Raman analysis of the o-GA and t-GA. It is found that o-GA is showing five distinguished modes of vibration at ~3410, ~2923, ~1732, ~1595 and ~1211 cm−1 correspond to the O–H, -C-H, –C(O)–O, CC and C–O bond stretching, respectively as shown in Fig. 2 a (black line). While after functionalization with thiourea (Fig. 2a, green line), t-GA is showing mainly nine different characteristic modes of vibrations observed at ~3434, ~2958, ~1720, ~1639, ~1586, ~1404, ~1240, ~1098 and ~620 cm−1 related to O–H/N–H, -C-H, –C(O)–O, –C(O)–N, CC, C–N, C–O, CS (asymmetric) (Qu et al., 2013) and CS (symmetric) bond, respectively(Chauhan et al., 2019). It can be clearly seen that the O–H peak is more broadened due to the insertion of N–H bonds which lead to more solubility in water. Also, the intensity of -C-H bonds is increased because of the generation of various defects on the surface of t-GA after functionalization(Gunture et al., 2020). It is observed that the intensity of C(O)–O bond was decreased due to participation in amide bonds. Additionally, two new peaks were also observed due to C–N and CS bonds, that were incorporated after the process of functionalization. Similarly, the Raman analysis also supports the functionalization, as it is found that Raman spectra of o-GA is showing two well-known D and G bands at ~1346 and ~1592 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2b, black line). While in the case of t-GA, D and G bands are observed at ~1339 and ~1576 cm−1, respectively, which shows that sp2 framework of o-GA was weakened and after the functionalization which led to the shifting of both D and G bands towards lower wavenumber (Mallakpour et al., 2014) (Fig. 2b, green line). It is also observed that the ID/IG ratio of o-GA is found to be ~2.54 and after functionalization, it increased to ~2.94 (Sapner et al., 2020).

The optical properties of t-GA have been interpreted using UV–Vis and fluorescence analysis. UV–Vis spectra of t-GA show peaks (as shown in Fig. 3 a, black line) at ~235 and ~258 nm which correspond to the π→π* electronic transitions of CC bonds of sp2 graphitic framework and CO bond, respectively. Also, an extra broad peak is observed at ~350 nm which can be related to the n→π* transition of CO/C(O)–N and CS bonds that confirms the surface functionalization(Jin et al., 2018). Fig. 3a, the green line is the fluorescence spectra of t-GA showing maximum emission at ~527 nm (yellow-green region) at an excitation wavelength of ~467 nm. The fluorescence of t-GA has been analyzed in the continuous light irradiation for 3 h, and it was observed that t-GA was showing excellent fluorescence photostability (Fig. 3b). The inset of Fig. 3b is showing daylight and UV-light images of t-GA filled in the cuvette showing bright yellow fluorescence in UV light. Fig. 3c shows the fluorescence emission spectra of t-GA observed at different excitation wavelengths varying in the range of (300–480) nm. It is found that t-GA was showing almost an excitation-independent emission behavior. Various excitation centers are also being analyzed by giving emissions at 527 nm. It was found that t-GA shows two excitation centers located at ~2.65 and ~3.54 eV (also appeared in UV–vis spectra) Fig. 3d. Further, the yellow-fluorescence of t-GA was used to analyze for the selective sensing of toxic metal ions.

The structure and morphology of t-GA have been investigated by the Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis. Fig. 4 a represents the low-resolution TEM showing various large sheet-like structures. Fig. 4b is showing the low-resolution TEM image of t-GA in which various crumbled and rotated sheet-like structures can be seen. The high-resolution TEM analysis in Fig. 4c, represents the graphitic fringes of t-GA and surface defects which could be responsible for the emissive behavior of t-GA(Singh et al., 2017). Fig. 4d is the another HRTEM image of t-GA showing graphitic fringes of interplanar d-spacing ~0.31 nm and presence of differentially shaped graphitic patches (shown by yellow circles).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing of Pb(II) metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selective sensing of the specific Pb(II) metal ion from the tested many other metal ions has been performed based on the fluorescence quenching experiment of t-GA in aqueous media. The quenching experiments were performed at the maximum emission of t-GA (~527 nm) at an excitation wavelength of ~467 nm. From Fig. 5 a it can be evidenced that the fluorescence intensity of t-GA is completely quenched in the case of Pb(II) ions as compared to other metal ions. Fig. 5b is showing the relative fluorescence intensity of t-GA with different metal ions showing selective fluorescence quenching with Pb(II) metal ions. Although some other soft metals also showed the fluorescence quenching, but maximum quenching was observed in case of Pb(II). Also, for naked eye visualization, selective fluorescence quenching is analyzed by taking a photographic picture of t-GA with different metal ions, where Pb(II) solution with t-GA is showing no fluorescence as compared to other metal ions as shown in Fig. 5c. Fig. 5d is showing the gradual decrease in fluorescence of t-GA with Pb(II) metal ions titration (in the range 0–720 μM). A well-known Stern-Volmer (Chauhan et al., 2019) plot is also plotted to get the linear relationship of Pb(II) ions concentration and fluorescence intensity as shown in Fig. 5e. It was also observed that the Stern-Volmer plot was well fitted in the experimental data showing a good correlation coefficient (~0.99). The value of Stern Volmer constant KSV is found to be 0.0008189 nM−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was also calculated and found to be ~7.3 nM.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of t-GA has also been checked. It is observed that t-GA is showing maximum fluorescence at neutral pH, while at acidic and basic pH, fluorescence intensity decreased attributed to either opening of amide bond or the interaction of H+ and OH− ions with surface functional groups. Also, it was observed that after the addition of the equal amount of Pb(II) metal ions (1 mM) at neutral and basic pH, fluorescence is completely quenched while at acidic pH, there may be competition between H+ and Pb(II) metal ions (Zhong et al., 2020) and hence fluorescence is not completely quenched.

The ability of t-GA of selective fluorescence quenching towards Pb(II) metal ions can be explained by three different types of mechanistic features i.e. (1) electron transfer from t-GA to vacant d-orbital of Pb(II) metal ions, known as LMCT (Rani et al., 2016), (2) Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) (Han et al., 2010) and (3) Inner Filter Effect (IFE) (Chen et al., 2017). To get the favorable mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching, emission and excitation spectra of t-GA have been plotted along with the absorbance of Pb(II) and t-GA. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6 a that the absorbance of Pb(II) metal ions does not intersect at any point with either the emission or with the excitation of t-GA which is the desired condition for FRET and IFE fluorescence quenching mechanism(Chen et al., 2018). So, the photons responsible for the excitation and emission of t-GA are not absorbed by an absorber (i.e. Pb(II) metal ions). Thus, LMCT was found to be the most responsible mechanism for the selective fluorescence quenching. It can also be noted that t-GA is having various active sulfur-containing sufacial functional groups which simply can bind with the soft metal ions like Pb(II) which leads to the formation of a non-fluorescent metal-ligand complex. After the excitation of electrons to the LUMO of t-GA, energy in the form of electrons could be transferred to the vacant d-orbital of Pb(II) metal ions, which thus retarding the de-excitation process (responsible for fluorescence emission) as shown in Fig. 6b. Fig. 6c is showing the schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching of t-GA by interaction of Pb(II) metal ions with thiourea group. The fluorescence quenching mechanism has also been supported by FTIR analysis. For the same, a detailed comparative FTIR analysis of t-GA before and after adding Pb(II) metal ions was performed, as shown in Figs. S1(a and b). It can clearly be evidenced that stretching vibrations related to the CS group at ~1103 and ~611 cm−1 are red-shifted to ~1097 and ~602 cm−1 after adding Pb(II) metal ions. These interactions indicate the most probable binding site of Pb(II) metal ions in the thio group (-CS), whose stretching has mostly been affected. The shifting of important peaks of t-GA after and before the addition of Pb(II) is summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry study for sensing Pb(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get some insights into the selective sensing of Pb(II) by t-GA, the electrochemical behavior has been studied by cyclic voltammetry based experiments (Thakur et al., 2013; Lim et al., 2015; Moutcine et al., 2020) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The cyclic voltammogram for selective sensing of Pb(II) by t-GA is shown in Fig. 7 . In an aqueous solution, t-GA showed redox responses at E Pa = −0.78 V with +0.13 mA peak current vs. Ag/AgCl and E Pc = −0.025 V with −0.06 mA peak current vs. Ag/AgCl. The redox responses recorded after the addition of Pb(II) metal ions, at different concentrations were observed using the electrochemical sensing system in the aqueous phase. After the addition of a 100 μM aqueous solution of Pb(II) ions, two irreversible redox responses were observed at E 1/2 = −0.37 V vs. Ag/AgCl, E 1/2 = 0.29 V vs. Ag/AgCl and one additional redox peak appeared at E Pc = −0.035 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The redox peak intensity increased on increasing the concentration of the Pb(II) metal ions from 100 μM to 200 μM (as shown in Fig. 7). Thus cyclic voltammetry experiments support the sensing of Pb(II) metal ions.

To explore the further applicability of fluorescent t-GA, sensing of commonly used hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was also performed. t-GA is showing fluorescence quenching after the addition of 30% H2O2, while there is a slight decrement in the case of the same amount of H2O as shown in Fig. 8 a. In principle, H2O2 generates OH● in presence of photons, which could oxidize the surface functional groups (thiourea to thiourea dioxide) and thus quenching the fluorescence(Makarov et al., 2014; Chaudhary et al., 2016; Siddiqui et al., 2020). A fluorescence-based titration is also performed to check the effect of concentration of H2O2 on fluorescence as shown in Fig. 8b. It can be seen that with the addition of small aliquots of H2O2, fluorescence intensity was gradually decreasing. Fig. 8c is the linear fitted Stern Volmer plot of relative fluorescence intensity versus concentration of H2O2 with a correlation coefficient of ~0.9829 and Stern-Volmer constant found to be ~0.00247 μM−1. The LOD was found to be as lower as ~2.42 μM. The inset of Fig. 8c is the photographic images of 2 ml of t-GA before and after the addition of 0.5 ml of 30% H2O2 taken under the illumination of UV light. It was found that t-GA showed a linear range (~0–0.1) mM of fluorescence sensing. The fluorescence quenching in the case of H2O2 has been supported by a comparative FTIR analysis as shown in Figs. S1(c and d), which shows that the addition of H2O2 resulted in the shifting of the existing peaks and emergence of few new peaks (Table S1). It was observed that the stretching of the CS group is red-shifted from ~1103 cm−1 to ~1083 cm−1. Also, three new peaks have appeared at ~1381 cm−1, ~957 cm−1, and ~526 cm−1. These new peaks could be related to the sulphoxilic group HO–SO and S–O bonds formed after the oxidation of thiourea to thiourea dioxide, respectively.

Based on the above results, t-GA have been proved as an efficient sensor for Pb(II) metal ions and H2O2 in water. The sensing-based applications of t-GA are also found to be comparable with the recently reported sensors and tabulated in Table 1 .

The sensing ability of t-GA has been further checked by performing quantification studies. For this, the fluorescence emission of t-GA and some unknown concentrations of Pb(II) ions and H2O2 were analyzed. After using the Stern-Volmer constants of respective quenchers, unknown concentrations are calculated and compared with the actual concentrations. The results are summarized in the following comparative bar plot (Fig. 9 a, b). Based on the results, our sensor can be accurately responsive for small concentrations of Pb(II) ions and H2O2. But for large concentrations, the experimentally calculated values are relatively less than actual concentrations, which is the topic of further research.

The selective fluorescence quenching of Pb(II) and H2O2 has also been analyzed in different wastewater samples by the method of external spiking to check its capability in real-life analysis. For the same, t-GA solutions were prepared in de-ionized (DI) water (S0), tap water (S1), B. Tech. Laboratory wastewater (S2) and two different industrial wastewater samples (S3, S4). It is observable from Fig. 9c that, even after the involvements of unknown industrial samples, t-GA showed sufficient fluorescence emission compared to DI water. However, some quenching was observed may be because of various other impurities and highly acidic contents of the unknown samples, which can retard the process of excitation and emission of t-GA. After that, fluorescence quenching analysis is performed in these samples, and results are shown in the bar plot (Fig. 9d). It can be seen that in all the samples, after the addition of Pb(II) (100 μM)) and H2O2 (1.76 mM), the fluorescence emission is fully and partially quenched, respectively.

The yellow fluorescent t-GA has also been used as a fluorescent ink for printing purposes due to having good solubility and wash ability with water. For the same, a dilute solution of t-GA was prepared without using any extra chemicals and then the alphabets ‘LEAD’ was write down using a brush dipped with the t-GA solution on a TLC plate. Then the photographic images of the TLC plate were captured in presence of normal daylight and UV light as shown in Fig. 10 (a and b). It can be observed that t-GA can also be used as a fluorescent ink material (Atchudan et al., 2018) for security purposes, document writings, stamps, etc. for data encryption and anticounterfeiting (Garg et al., 2020).

A simple synthetic protocol involving the functionalization of GA via the DCC coupling has been carried out here to get t-GA. The surface functionalization by thiourea imparts the high degree of surface passivation, resulting in good solubility and photostability in the aqueous medium. The t-GA is proved to be an efficient fluorescent probe for the selective sensing of toxic Pb(II) metal ions by the process of fluorescence quenching with a lower LOD of ~7.3 nM. The cyclic voltammetry studies support the fluorescence-based sensing. The detailed mechanism of fluorescence quenching has been discussed based on the absorption and emission profiles. Also, t-GA is showing good sensing ability towards H2O2 with a LOD of ~2.42 μM. Further, the fluorescence-based application of t-GA is more explored, and it is found that a dilute solution of t-GA could be used as a fluorescent ink in UV light for various purposes.

Graphene aerogel (GA) was synthesized by previously reported method (Myung et al., 2019). Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) (99%), thiourea (98%), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) (~25% as ammonia), tetra-n-butyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) (98%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the chosen metal salts, nitric acid (HNO3), acetonitrile, acetone, ethanol, Rhodamine 6G were of analytical grade and used without any additional purification. Deionized water was bought from Vision Ultrapure company. 30% w/v H2O2 (extrapure) was purchased from FINAR and used without further purification.

The oxidized version o-GAwas prepared from the previously reported method(Myung et al., 2019; Kaushik et al., 2021). Briefly, 200 mg of GA was oxidized by refluxing with 50 ml of ~7.8 M HNO3 solution in a RB flask for 6 h at 140 °C. The o-GA solution was then centrifuged, and the residue was washed with DI water. After that it was dried on a water bath to remove residual nitrate ions. Griess Reagent test was used to check whether the excess of nitrate ions are removed or not. The final dried powder was collected yielding ~84% of o-GA.

Then 100 mg of o-GA was dispersed in 20 ml water via sonication in a RB flask. Then, 80 mg of (DCC) was transferred to the solution. It was then stirred for 30 min followed by sonication for 30 min. Meanwhile, 8 mg of thiourea was dissolved in 80 ml of DI water in a 100 ml beaker and the pH of the solution was set to ~10.4 using ammonium hydroxide solution. This basic solution was then poured slowly into the RB flask containing o-GA and DCC mixture with stirring. After that, this solution was refluxed at ~90 °C for 24 h. Then, the reaction mixture was filtered and the solid residue was washed with acetonitrile, acetone and ethanol to remove unreacted DCC. The residue obtained was then dried in the oven at 60 °C for a whole night. The final sample obtained was then named as thiourea functionalized graphene aerogel (t-GA) which was found to be bright yellow fluorescent in an aqueous medium. After that, dilute solutions of t-GA were prepared for various fluorescence studies. The quantum yield of t-GA was calculated by taking Rhodamine 6G in ethanol as a reference (QY = ~95%) and was found to be ~3% (Lu et al., 2018).

A 0.25 mg ml−1 concentrated solution of t-GA was prepared for metal ion selectivity and fluorescence quenching experiments. Selective fluorescence quenching was checked by adding ~0.95 mM of different metal ion solutions to the t-GA solution and fluorescence spectra were observed. A concentration of 0–720 μM of Pb(II) was added in small aliquots to 2 ml solution of t-GA to get, the effect of concentration on fluorescence intensity. The electrochemical responses were collected on a 3 electrode setup. The setup contained a Pt electrode as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and a Pt wire as an auxiliary electrode. For electrochemical metal sensing, 0.25 mg ml−1 solution of t-GA was prepared. A 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) solution was also prepared and used as the supporting electrolyte, and the response was collected at a fixed scan rate of 50 mV s−1. For the sensing of H2O2, 30% w/v H2O2 is used without any dilution. For the comparative analysis, in 1 ml of DI water and 30% w/v H2O2 was added to 1 ml of 0.25 mg ml−1 aqueous solution of t-GA. While for fluorescence titration experiments, H2O2 was added in aliquots to 2 ml of 0.25 mg ml−1 aqueous solution of t-GA and fluorescence spectra were recorded after every aliquot.

The quantification of unknown sample was carried out by the addition of different amount of Pb(II) ions and H2O2 in t-GA samples and emission spectra were collected. For real sample analysis, a 0.25 mg ml−1 solution of t-GA was prepared in six different wastewater samples namely de-ionized (DI) water (S0), tap water (S1), and industrail wastewater collected from sanganer industry on differetn days (S3, S4) and emission spectra were recorded for each sample. After that, 100 μM of Pb(II) and 500 μL of 30% H2O2 solution was made in these samples by the method of external spiking and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded.

A dilute aqueous solution of 0.25 mg ml−1 of t-GA was prepared then the alphabetic letters ‘‘LEAD’’ were painted on a TLC plate using a brush. Then, photographic images of the TLC plate were captured in daylight and under the UV light.

XPS analysis was performed on ESCA+ omicron nanotechnology oxford instrument. The FTIR spectra were observed on a BrukerVertex 70 FT-IR spectrophotometer using pressed KBr pellets. Raman spectra were obtained from a confocal Raman spectrometer at 532 nm laser excitation. The surface morphology of t-GA was observed by using a Tecnai 20 G2 300 kV, STWIN model TEM and HRTEM was observed at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were taken from Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer at room temperature in the aqueous medium. Fluorescence spectra analysis was performed by using a Fluoromax 4C.L instrument. Electrochemical studies were performed on K-Lyte 1.2 model from Kanopy Techno Solutions Pvt. Ltd. Electrochemical workstation.

S. K. S. and K. M. T. planned and supervised all experiments of the presented work; J. K. and G. performed all the experimental works. R.S. performed the cyclic voltametry related experiments. All authors reviewed the manuscript.
